front: What is a group in abstract algebra?
back: A group (G, *) is a set G, closed under a binary operation *, such that the following axioms hold: 1) Associativity: (a*b)*c = a*(b*c) for all a,b,c in G. 2) Identity: There exists an element e in G such that e*a = a*e = a for all a in G. 3) Inverse: For each a in G, there is an element a⁻¹ in G such that a*a⁻¹ = a⁻¹*a = e.

front: What is an abelian (or commutative) group?
back: A group (G, *) is abelian if, in addition to the group axioms, its operation is commutative. That is, a*b = b*a for every a and b in the group G. Examples include (ℤ, +) and (ℝ\{0}, ×).

front: What is the order of a group and the order of an element?
back: The order of a group G, denoted |G|, is the number of elements in G. The order of an element a in a group is the smallest positive integer m such that aᵐ = e (the identity). If no such integer exists, the element has infinite order.

A subset H of a group G is a subgroup of G if H is itself a group under the operation inherited from G. This is true if and only if: 1) H is closed under the group operation. 2) The identity element e of G is in H. 3) H is closed under taking inverses.
A group G is cyclic if there exists an element a in G (called a generator) such that every element of G is a power of a (i.e., G = {aⁿ | n ∈ ℤ}). The cyclic group generated by a is denoted ⟨a⟩.
